items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.
Correct answer: IP addresses. “Pictures” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is the secure web protocol?
items.why: Correct answer: HTTPS. “HTTP” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many numbers separated by dots are in a normal IPv4 address?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
